title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Hot and Sour Soup, garnished with green onions and chili oil.
seo.imageCaption: Warm up with a delicious bowl of our homemade Hot and Sour Soup – a classic Chinese favorite!

description: Hot and Sour Soup is a beloved Chinese classic, known for its invigorating blend of spicy heat and tangy sourness. This comforting soup features a rich, savory broth brimming with tender protein, earthy mushrooms, and silky tofu, all balanced with a subtle sweetness. It's the perfect dish to warm you from the inside out and awaken your taste buds with every spoonful.

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in separate bowls of hot water for 20-30 minutes until soft. Squeeze out excess water, remove shiitake stems, and slice both types of mushrooms thinly. Drain and slice tofu and bamboo shoots into matchsticks. Thinly slice the pork or chicken into matchsticks.

instructions.title: Cook the Protein
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a tablespoon of cooking oil over medium-high heat. Add the sliced pork or chicken and stir-fry until it is no longer pink, about 3-4 minutes. Remove the cooked meat and set aside, leaving any rendered fat in the pot.

instructions.title: Simmer Broth and Vegetables
instructions.text: Pour the chicken broth into the pot. Add the rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, sliced bamboo shoots, and tofu. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Season the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the rice vinegar, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and white pepper into the simmering soup. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed, adding more vinegar or pepper for desired hot and sour balance. In a small bowl, whisk together the cornstarch and 1/4 cup cold water to create a slurry.

instructions.title: Thicken and Add Egg Swirl
instructions.text: Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup while continuously stirring to prevent lumps. The soup should begin to thicken within a minute or two. Once thickened, slowly drizzle the beaten eggs into the soup in a thin stream while gently stirring with chopsticks or a fork to create delicate egg ribbons.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the pot from the heat. Stir in the toasted sesame oil and the cooked pork or chicken. Ladle the hot and sour soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with sliced green onions and a drizzle of chili oil if desired for extra heat. Serve immediately.

tips: For a vegetarian version, substitute pork with extra firm tofu or rehydrated gluten, and use vegetable broth instead of chicken broth.
Adjust the amount of rice vinegar and white pepper to your personal preference for sourness and spiciness. A little extra chili oil can boost the heat.
If you prefer a thicker soup, you can add a little more cornstarch slurry, but do so gradually to avoid an overly thick consistency.
article:
Mastering the Art of Hot and Sour Soup: A Culinary Journey

Hot and Sour Soup, a beloved staple of Chinese cuisine, is a dish that tantalizes the taste buds with its complex symphony of flavors. It’s a comforting yet invigorating bowl, known for its distinctive blend of spicy heat, tangy sourness, savory umami, and a satisfyingly thick texture. Whether you’re a seasoned chef or a home cook looking to explore the rich tapestry of Chinese soups and stews, understanding the nuances of this classic will undoubtedly elevate your culinary skills. This article will guide you through everything you need to know to create an authentic and delicious Hot and Sour Soup recipe right in your own kitchen.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

Hot and Sour Soup, or Suan La Tang in Mandarin, is a quintessential Chinese soup whose origins are often traced back to Sichuan or Hunan provinces, though variations exist across many regional cuisines. It's characterized by its dual dominant flavors: the "hot" comes from white pepper and often chili oil, while the "sour" is derived primarily from vinegar, typically black rice vinegar. Beyond these primary notes, the soup offers a deep savory base, usually from a rich broth, complemented by an assortment of ingredients that add texture and depth. Common additions include slivers of pork or chicken, rehydrated dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and silken tofu. The soup is traditionally thickened with a cornstarch slurry, giving it a velvety consistency, and finished with a swirl of egg ribbons and a drizzle of sesame oil. It's a dish that warms you from the inside out, stimulating the palate with every spoonful.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting the perfect Hot and Sour Soup recipe begins with selecting the right ingredients. Here’s a comprehensive list to get you started:

*   **For the Broth:** 6 cups chicken or vegetable broth (high quality makes a big difference!)
*   **Protein (Optional):** 4 oz lean pork loin or chicken breast, cut into thin matchsticks
*   **Mushrooms:** 1/2 oz dried shiitake mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced; 1/2 oz dried wood ear mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced
*   **Vegetables:** 1/2 cup bamboo shoots, thinly sliced; 1/2 cup firm or silken tofu, cut into small cubes
*   **Aromatics:** 2 cloves garlic, minced; 1 inch ginger, grated
*   **Flavor Powerhouses:** 1/4 cup Chinese black vinegar (Chinkiang vinegar is ideal); 2-3 tablespoons soy sauce (light); 1-2 teaspoons white pepper (adjust to taste for heat); 1/2-1 teaspoon chili oil (optional, for extra heat)
*   **Thickener:** 3 tablespoons cornstarch mixed with 1/4 cup cold water (cornstarch slurry)
*   **Egg:** 1 large egg, lightly beaten
*   **Finishing Touches:** 1 teaspoon sesame oil; 2 tablespoons chopped green onions (for garnish)

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Follow these steps to bring your delicious Hot and Sour Soup recipe to life:

1.  **Prepare Ingredients:** If using dried mushrooms, rehydrate them in hot water for about 20-30 minutes until soft. Squeeze out excess water and slice thinly. Slice pork/chicken, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Prepare your cornstarch slurry and lightly beat the egg.
2.  **Sauté Aromatics:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a tablespoon of oil over medium heat. Add the minced garlic and grated ginger, and sauté for about 30 seconds until fragrant. If using pork or chicken, add it now and stir-fry until lightly browned, about 2-3 minutes.
3.  **Add Broth and Solids:** Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth. Bring to a gentle boil. Add the sliced shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and bamboo shoots. Reduce heat to a simmer and cook for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to meld.
4.  **Season the Soup:** Stir in the soy sauce, Chinese black vinegar, and white pepper. Taste and adjust seasonings – you might want more vinegar for tang or more white pepper for heat. If desired, add chili oil at this stage for an extra kick.
5.  **Add Tofu and Thicken:** Gently add the cubed tofu. Bring the soup back to a gentle simmer. Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. You may not need all of the slurry.
6.  **Swirl in Egg:** Once the soup is thickened, remove the pot from direct heat or lower the heat to its absolute minimum. Slowly drizzle the lightly beaten egg into the hot soup in a thin stream, stirring gently with chopsticks or a fork in a circular motion. This will create beautiful, delicate egg ribbons.
7.  **Finish and Serve:** Stir in the sesame oil. Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions. Serve immediately and enjoy the fruits of your labor!

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving the perfect balance in your Hot and Sour Soup recipe requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Quality Broth is Key:** A rich, flavorful broth forms the foundation of this soup. Homemade chicken broth is ideal, but a good quality store-bought low-sodium broth will also work well.
*   **Don't Skimp on White Pepper:** White pepper is crucial for the "hot" element. It provides a unique, pungent heat that differs from chili spice. Don't be afraid to add more than you think you need, then taste and adjust.
*   **Balance the Sourness:** Chinese black vinegar is the star for the "sour." Add it gradually and taste. Different brands have varying intensities. You want a pronounced tang, but not so much that it overpowers the other flavors.
*   **Perfecting the Thickness:** The cornstarch slurry is your friend. Ensure the water is cold when mixing the cornstarch to prevent lumps. Add it slowly, stirring constantly, until the soup reaches a velvety, not gloopy, consistency.
*   **Egg Ribbons:** For delicate egg ribbons, ensure the soup is hot but not boiling rapidly when you drizzle in the egg. Stir gently and continuously to prevent the egg from clumping.
*   **Freshness Matters:** While many ingredients are dried, using fresh ginger and garlic will significantly boost the aromatic profile.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is a versatile dish that can be enjoyed in many ways:

*   **As an Appetizer:** It’s a fantastic starter for any Chinese meal, whetting the appetite with its vibrant flavors.
*   **With Rice or Noodles:** A bowl of Hot and Sour Soup paired with steamed white rice or a side of plain noodles makes for a light yet satisfying meal.
*   **Part of a Larger Meal:** Serve it alongside other Chinese dishes like fried rice, spring rolls, dumplings, or stir-fries for a complete culinary experience.
*   **Garnish Variations:** Besides green onions, consider a sprinkle of fresh cilantro, a few drops of extra chili oil, or even some crispy fried shallots for added texture and flavor.

## Variations and Substitutions

This adaptable Hot and Sour Soup recipe allows for numerous tweaks to suit dietary preferences or ingredient availability:

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Omit the pork/chicken and use vegetable broth. Ensure your soy sauce is vegan (some brands contain wheat). For a vegan version, skip the egg or replace it with a "flax egg" (though it won't create ribbons). Extra mushrooms or sliced rehydrated dried bean curd can add more substance.
*   **Protein Alternatives:** Instead of pork, you can use thinly sliced chicken breast, shrimp, or even shredded duck.
*   **Mushroom Variety:** Experiment with other dried mushrooms like oyster mushrooms or even fresh cremini mushrooms if wood ear or shiitake are hard to find.
*   **Spice Level:** For more heat, increase the amount of white pepper or chili oil. A pinch of dried red chili flakes can also be added. For less heat, reduce the white pepper.
*   **Sourness:** While black vinegar is traditional, rice vinegar can be used in a pinch, though it will yield a slightly different flavor profile.
*   **Extra Vegetables:** Feel free to add other quick-cooking vegetables like shredded carrots, snow peas, or water chestnuts for extra crunch and nutrition.

Embracing the rich flavors and textures of Hot and Sour Soup is a rewarding experience. This comprehensive Hot and Sour Soup recipe provides you with all the tools to recreate this classic Chinese dish, allowing you to customize it to your heart's content. So, gather your ingredients, follow these steps, and prepare to delight your senses with a bowl of truly authentic and incredibly satisfying Hot and Sour Soup. Enjoy the journey of bringing this culinary masterpiece to your table!
